sections.title: Preparing for the Mela
sections.content_summary: Nita, Radha, and their Dadiji prepare for an outing to the mela, which is far enough to require a bus ride. The preparations involve packing, filling water bottles, coordinating with neighbours Sneha and Rohit, and waiting for Mohan Chacha's family arriving by train. The section highlights inclusive community planning — including persuading an elderly, mobility-limited Dadiji to join — and shows the extended family and neighbours functioning as a single social unit for outings.

tasks.task_text: How do you prepare yourself for a trip?

sections.title: On the Way to the Mela
sections.content_summary: The group walks carefully to the bus stop observing road safety, boards bus number 401, and the conductor helps Dadiji to a reserved seat for older people. Rohit's father buys tickets for everyone and instructs children not to lean out of the window. The section introduces different modes of transport used in a city (bus, train, autorickshaw) and models road safety, bus etiquette, and caring for elders during travel.

tasks.task_text: Describe briefly any trip that you have taken with your friends, neighbours or family.
What transport do you take to travel within your city/town/village?

sections.title: At the Mela
sections.content_summary: The mela at the parade ground features a map at the entrance showing stall locations, a Lost and Found booth, a Help Booth, First Aid, and emergency vehicles (ambulance, police jeep, fire engine) — introducing community safety infrastructure. Children enjoy stalls with traditional toys (spinning tops, puppets, phirkis, dolls), rides (merry-go-round, giant wheel), a magic show, and a wide variety of Indian street foods (gol gappas, chaat, chhole kulche, jalebis with rabri, kulfi). The section emphasises hygiene (handwashing before eating) and civic responsibility (disposing waste in dustbins). A 'Do You Know' box introduces the Kumbh Mela as the world's largest gathering, held every 12 years at Haridwar, Prayagraj, Nashik, and Ujjain.

tasks.task_text: What is the purpose of the 'Lost and Found' booth?
What is the role of the police dog?
Have you ever been to a mela? What are the things you liked most in the mela?
Imagine yourself in place of Nita, Radha, Sneha and Rohit. Share with the class the interesting things you would do in the mela.
Talk to the elders in the family and find out how melas were different when they were young.
Why are fire engines and ambulances present in a mela?

Study the sketch and answer: Find and circle Rohit and Sneha's house on the map. Find and circle Nita and Radha's house on the map. Whose house is closer to the parade ground? If you have to go from the police station to the parade ground, which places would you pass through?
In the given space, sketch the route from home to school that you follow everyday.

sections.title: Let us reflect
sections.content_summary: This consolidation section has four sub-tasks: three discussion prompts on caring for elders, types of vehicles, and travel safety; a matching activity linking pictures of mela roles (firefighter, halwai, toy seller, puppeteer, bangle seller, volunteer, police officer, medical attendant, mehendi artist) to words; a drawing task of vehicles seen near school and home; and a role-play activity in which students organise a classroom mock mela using locally available materials.

tasks.task_text: How can we care for our elders?
What kinds of vehicles do we see around us?
How can we ensure safety during travel?
Pick a suitable word from the list, match it with a picture, and write the word below the picture. One has been done for you.
Draw and label different vehicles that you see around your school and home.

With the help of your teacher, organise a mock mela in your classroom. Discuss and plan the different roles, stalls, and games that are part of a mela. Use learning-teaching materials available locally, e.g., toys, puppets, play money, miniatures, and clay models to set up your stalls and role-play buyers and sellers.

dual_strand.natural: Movement through space — modes of transport, routes, and navigating the built environment safely
dual_strand.human_cultural: The mela as a community institution — traditional toys, street foods, performing arts (magic show, puppet show), community helpers, and intergenerational participation
